3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: A restaurant chain has three restaurants, Resto A, Resto B, and Resto C. The most recent financial statements for these restaurants show that all three restaurants have been profitable, as shown below. Resto A Resto B Resto C Total Operating income $ 210,000 $ 110,000 $ 260,000 $ 580,000 Interest on long-term 20,000 10,000 50,000 80,000 debt at 10% Income before $ 190,000 $ 100,000 $ 210,000 $ 500,000 income taxes Net income $ 133,000 $ 70,000 $ 147,000 $ 350,000 Current assets $ 200,000 $ 100,000 $ 500,000 $ 800,000 Total liabilities and $1,500,000 $1,000,000 $1,700,000 $4,200,000 equity The company requires a return on investment (ROI) of 13%, 10%, and 16% for Resto A, Resto B, and Resto C, respectively. During the past five years, the company as a whole has been earning an ROI of 15% and intends to maintain this percentage of return. Management’s primary focus for the foreseeable future is to generate cash flows. Which one of the following statements regarding financial performance of the restaurants is true?
o: Resto A and Resto B are not performing in line with the company ROI requirements because they have residual incomes of ($5,000) and $0, respectively
Resto A and Resto B are not performing in line with the company ROI requirements because they have residual incomes of ($36,000) and ($20,000), respectively
Resto C is not performing in line with the company ROI requirements because it has residual income of ($12,000)
All three restaurants have positive residual incomes of $21,000, $10,000 and $18,000 for Resto A, B, and C, respectively
3-11.e: Residual income is: Operating income of business unit − Assets of business unit × required rate of return = Residual income Resto C's operating income is $260,000, and its total assets are $1,700,000. Its required rate of return on investment is 16%, and 16% of $1,700,000 is $272,000. Therefore, Resto C's residual income is $260,000 − $272,000, which equals $(12,000).
wrongWhy: The residual incomes for Resto A and Resto B are $15,000 and $10,000, respectively. Resto A: $210,000 − ($1,500,000 × 0.13) = $15,000 Resto B: $110,000 − ($1,000,000 × 0.10) = $10,000 Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The residual incomes for Resto A and Resto B are $15,000 and $10,000, respectively. Resto A: $210,000 − ($1,500,000 × 0.13) = $15,000 Resto B: $110,000 − ($1,000,000 × 0.10) = $10,000 Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The residual incomes for Resto A, Resto B, and Resto C are $15,000, $10,000, and $(12,000), respectively. Resto A: $210,000 − ($1,500,000 × 0.13) = $15,000 Resto B: $110,000 − ($1,000,000 × 0.10) = $10,000 Resto C: $260,000 − ($1,700,000 × 0.16) = $(12,000) Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 31.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: A company has recently implemented responsibility accounting in all 7 segments of the company. The following information is available for Segment W for the last quarter. Based on the information provided, if the company treats Segment W as an investment center, what is the annual return on investment for Segment W?
3-11.e: Annual return on investment is calculated as the annual return, or income, of the segment, divided by the assets invested in the segment. When the income and the assets are for one quarter, the income is annualized before dividing it by the quarter's invested assets. The income of the segment for the quarter is calculated as the revenues ($8,000,000) minus cost of sales ($6,350,000) minus general and administrative expenses ($180,000) for a quarterly return of $1,470,000. The annualized return is $1,470,000 × 4 = $5,880,000. The invested assets include net working capital ($12,000,000) and net property, plant and equipment ($31,750,000), for total invested assets of $43,750,000. Annual ROI is thus calculated as $5,880,000 ÷ $43,750,000 = 13.44%.
wrongWhy: Annual return on investment is calculated as the annual return, or income, of the segment, divided by the assets invested in the segment. When the income and the assets are for one quarter, the income should be annualized before dividing it by the quarter's invested assets. This answer results from not annualizing the segment's quarterly income before dividing it by the assets invested in the segment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Annual return on investment is calculated as the annual return, or income, of the segment, divided by the assets invested in the segment. When the income and the assets are for one quarter, the income is annualized before dividing it by the quarter's invested assets. This answer does not include the general and administrative expenses in the calculation of the annual return of the segment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Annual return on investment is calculated as the annual return, or income, of the segment, divided by the assets invested in the segment. When the income and the assets are for one quarter, the income is annualized before dividing it by the quarter's invested assets. This answer does not include the net working capital in the calculation of the amount invested in the segment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 32.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: The most likely reason a company would switch its performance measurement tool from ROI to RI is because
o: RI provides a basis to evaluate units with different sizes
3-11.e: Goal-congruency refers to the aligning of goals of the individual managers with the goals of the whole organization. An individual division manager may reject a capital investment that would improve the company’s total profits because the proposed project’s return on investment would cause his own division’s return on investment to decrease. But since the investment would improve the company’s overall profit, the proposed project should be accepted. If the expected rate of return on a new investment is greater than the required rate of return (usually the cost of capital), residual income (RI) will increase due to the new investment, even if the expected return on investment (ROI) for the new project is lower than the current return on investment. An investment that may have been rejected on the basis that its ROI was lower than the unit’s existing ROI would instead be accepted, and the company would benefit from the new investment. Therefore, when RI is used in preference to ROI, a project that would be beneficial to the company is more likely to be selected, even if its ROI is lower than the unit’s existing ROI, and goal-congruency will be better achieved.
wrongWhy: RI does not incorporate nonfinancial factors. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
RI does not provide a basis to evaluate units with different sizes. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
ROI does not favor large units. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 33.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: Residual income is often preferred over return on investment (ROI) as a performance evaluation measure because
o: the imputed interest rate used in calculating residual income is more easily derived than the target rate that is compared to the calculated ROI
residual income is a measure over time while ROI represents the results for a single time period
residual income concentrates on maximizing the amount of income rather than a percentage return as with ROI
average investment is employed with residual income while year-end investment is employed with ROI
3-11.e: Residual income is the operating income earned after the the required charge for the funds invested by the company in its operations (usually the cost of capital) has been covered. If the expected rate of return on a new investment is greater than the required rate of return, residual income will increase as a result of the new investment even if the expected return on investment (ROI) for the new project is lower than the division's current return on investment. When evaluating a potential project for investment, any project that has a positive RI will be accepted, even if it will reduce the overall company’s or unit’s ROI. Therefore, an investment that may have been rejected on the basis that its ROI was lower than the existing ROI would instead be accepted, and the company would benefit from the new investment.
wrongWhy: The imputed interest rate used in calculating residual income is the organization's required rate of return, and the same required rate of return is used to evaluate return on investment. The required rate of return is determined by senior management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Both residual income and return on investment are related to a single time period, but residual income is a monetary amount whereas return on investment is a percentage return amount. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Either average investment or year-end investment may be used to evaluate both residual income and return on investment. In practice, accountants use the average of the beginning and ending balances of the year for total assets in calculating ROI and residual income. However, HOCK has been informed by the ICMA that the CMA Part 1 exam questions will not require candidates to use an average of the employed assets of the business unit. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 34.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: A company has an 8% required rate of return. It is evaluating the following four mutually exclusive projects as possible investments. Estimated Operating Income Assets Using the residual income method, which one of the four projects should the company accept?
3-11.e: The company should accept the project with the highest estimated residual income, and Project B has the highest estimated residual income. The formula for residual income is: Operating income of the business unit Minus: (Assets of the business unit × Required rate of return) Equals: Residual Income Estimated residual income for Project B is: $1,500,000 − ($7,500,000 × 0.08) = $900,000
wrongWhy: The company should accept the project with the highest estimated residual income. This is not the project with the highest estimated residual income. The formula for residual income is: Operating income of the business unit Minus: (Assets of the business unit × Required rate of return) Equals: Residual Income Estimated residual income for Project D is: $750,000 − ($10,000,000 × 0.08) = $(50,000) Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The company should accept the project with the highest estimated residual income. This is not the project with the highest estimated residual income. The formula for residual income is: Operating income of the business unit Minus: (Assets of the business unit × Required rate of return) Equals: Residual Income Estimated residual income for Project C is: $850,000 − ($1,500,000 × 0.08) = $730,000 Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The company should accept the project with the highest estimated residual income. This is not the project with the highest estimated residual income. The formula for residual income is: Operating income of the business unit Minus: (Assets of the business unit × Required rate of return) Equals: Residual Income Estimated residual income for Project A is: $1,250,000 − ($5,000,000 × 0.08) = $850,000 Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 35.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: A company uses return on investment (ROI) to compare its divisions, using this evaluation to determine division manager bonuses. Which method of asset measurement would provide the best method of comparison?
3-11.e: The current cost method of measurement will provide the best basis of comparison.
wrongWhy: Depreciated cost is not a good basis to use for comparison because ROI will be impacted by the age of the assets in each of the divisions being compared.
Book value is not a good basis to use for comparison because ROI will be impacted by the age of the assets in each of the divisions being compared.
Historical cost is not a good basis to use for comparison because ROI will be impacted by the age of the assets in each of the divisions being compared. 36.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: Performance results for four geographic divisions of a manufacturing company are shown below. Target Return Actual Return Return Division on Investment on Investment on Sales The division with the best performance is
3-11.e: Division B's actual return on investment exceeded its target return on investment by the greatest amount, among the divisions that did exceed their targets. In addition, among the divisions that exceeded their targets, Division B had one of the highest returns on sales. (Division D had a higher return on sales, but Division D did not exceed its target return on investment.) Therefore, Division B is the division with the best overall performance.
wrongWhy: Division C's actual return on investment exceeded its target return on investment, but not by as much as some other divisions. Furthermore, its return on sales was not as great as that of some other divisions. Therefore, it cannot qualify as the division with the best performance.

Division A's actual return on investment exceeded its target return on investment and among the divisions that did exceed their targets, it had one of the highest returns on sales. However, it did not exceed its target return on investment by as much as some other divisions did. Therefore, it cannot qualify as the division with the best performance.

Division D's actual return on investment was below its target return on investment. Its return on sales was the highest of all the divisions, but since it did not meet its target return on investment, it cannot qualify as the division with the best performance. 37.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: A company is concerned that its divisional managers are not making decisions that are in the best interests of the overall corporation. In order to prevent this, the company should use a performance evaluation system that focuses on
3-11.e: When division managers are evaluated on the basis of residual income, they are more likely to embark upon projects that will be in the best interests of the overall corporation. Residual income is not a rate, but instead, it is an amount of income. As long as the expected net income before taxes from the project is greater than the target monetary return in (calculated as a percentage of invested capital), the project will be in the best interests of the overall corporation and it will also have positive residual income. Using residual income overcomes a weakness inherent in using return on investment for manager evaluation. If return on investment is used, a project that would have a higher return than the company's cost of capital and thus would be good for the company may be rejected by the division manager because its return on investment is lower than the division's current return on investment and thus it would bring the division's return on investment down.
wrongWhy: A performance evaluation system that focuses on operating income would not necessarily encourage divisional managers to make decisions that are in the best interests of the company.
A performance evaluation system that focuses on controllable costs would not necessarily encourage divisional managers to make decisions that are in the best interests of the company.
A performance evaluation system that focuses on flexible budget variances would not necessarily encourage divisional managers to make decisions that are in the best interests of the company. 38.

3-11.topic: Return on Investment and Residual Income
3-11.q: A manufacturing company with four divisions throughout the United States has the following information about the four divisions’ performance available for analysis: Eastern Western Northern Southern Division Division Division Division Division operating income $15 million $17 million $13 million $11 million Division working capital $30 million $55 million $30 million $21 million Division assets $83 million $113 million $65 million $92 million Division liabilities $50 million $44 million $34 million $55 million Division equity $33 million $69 million $31 million $37 million If the company uses the divisions’ Returns on Investment to compare the performance of the division managers, which division had the highest Return on Investment?
3-11.e: Return on Investment is annual operating income of the business unit divided by assets of the business unit. The Northern Division’s ROI was the highest. The ROIs for the individual divisions are as follows: Eastern Western Northern Southern Division Division Division Division Division operating income $15 million $17 million $13 million $11 million ÷ Division assets $83 million $113 million $65 million $92 million = Return on Investment 18% 15% 20% 12%
wrongWhy: If annual operating income divided by equity of each business unit were used as Return on Investment, the Eastern Division would have the highest ROI; but that is not the way Return on Investment is calculated. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

If annual operating income divided by working capital of each business unit were used as Return on Investment, the Southern Division would have the highest ROI; but that is not the way Return on Investment is calculated. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

If annual operating income divided by liabilities of each business unit were used as Return on Investment, the Western Division would have the highest ROI; but that is not the way Return on Investment is calculated. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 1.

3-12.q: Which of the following are examples of KPIs within the internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard?
o: Return on investment and number of innovations
Internal promotions and working environment feedback
Number of technical support inquiries and percentage of product defects
Lead generation rate and process downtime
3-12.e: The internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard includes innovations and improvements in products and services, operations, and customer service/support needed to create value for customers. Key performance indicators (KPIs) are measures of the organization’s performance in the critical success factors that are essential to its competitive advantage and therefore to its success. Number of technical support inquiries and percentage of product defects are both examples of key performance indicators in the internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard.
wrongWhy: The internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard includes innovations and improvements in products and services, operations, and customer service/support needed to create value for customers. Key performance indicators (KPIs) are measures of the organization’s performance in the critical success factors that are essential to its competitive advantage and therefore to its success. Since the internal process perspective includes innovations and improvements in products and services, number of innovations would be an example of a key performance indicator in the internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard. However, return on investment would be an example of a key performance indicator in the financial perspective, not the internal process perspective. The financial perspective focuses on the organization’s financial objectives and enables tracking of financial success and shareholder value. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard includes innovations and improvements in products and services, operations, and customer service/support needed to create value for customers. Key performance indicators (KPIs) are measures of the organization’s performance in the critical success factors that are essential to its competitive advantage and therefore to its success. Internal promotions and working environment feedback would be examples of key performance indicators in the organizational capacity/learning and growth perspective, not the internal process perspective. The organizational capacity/learning and growth perspective of the balanced scorecard includes human capital, or the skills, talents, and knowledge of employees; information capital, or the information systems, networks, and technology infrastructure of the company; and organizational capital, or the company’s culture, leadership, degree of teamwork, and knowledge management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard includes innovations and improvements in products and services, operations, and customer service/support needed to create value for customers. Key performance indicators (KPIs) are measures of the organization’s performance in the critical success factors that are essential to its competitive advantage and therefore to its success. Process downtime is an example of a key performance indicator in the internal process perspective of a balanced scorecard, but lead generation rate is not. Lead generation rate would be an example of a key performance indicator in the customer perspective instead. The customer perspective involves identifying the market segment or segments the company wants to target and then measuring its success in those segments. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 2.

3-12.q: Which one of the following should be used for evaluating the performance of the Repair and Maintenance Department that repairs production equipment in a firm devoted to making keyboards for computers?
o: The variance between the firm's budgeted and actual net income
The response time and degree of satisfaction among the production departments
3-12.e: The "customers" of the Repair and Maintenance Department are internal. They are the users of the production equipment that the Repair and Maintenance Department is responsible for keeping in good working order. The response time and degree of satisfaction among the Repair and Maintenance Department's customers is an appropriate measurement for evaluating the performance of the Repair and Maintenance Department.
wrongWhy: The "customers" of the Repair and Maintenance Department are internal. They are the users of the production equipment that the Repair and Maintenance Department is responsible for keeping in good working order. Although the Repair and Maintenance Department may be indirectly responsible for an impact on net income if it does not do its job and does not keep the production machinery running, the Repair and Maintenance Department is not responsible for activities that directly impact the variance between the firm's budgeted and actual net income. Performance measures that measure the department's performance more directly should be used.

The "customers" of the Repair and Maintenance Department are internal. They are the users of the production equipment that the Repair and Maintenance Department is responsible for keeping in good working order. Although the Repair and Maintenance Department may be indirectly responsible for an impact on factory overhead costs if it does not do its job and does not keep the production machinery running, the Repair and Maintenance Department is not responsible for activities that directly impact the fixed overhead volume variances or any other factory variances. Performance measures that measure the department's performance more directly should be used.

The "customers" of the Repair and Maintenance Department are internal. They are the users of the production equipment that the Repair and Maintenance Department is responsible for keeping in good working order. Although the Repair and Maintenance Department may be indirectly responsible for an impact on factory overhead costs if it does not do its job and does not keep the production machinery running, the Repair and Maintenance Department is not responsible for activities that directly impact the total factory overhead variances or any other factory variances. Performance measures that measure the department's performance more directly should be used. 3.

3-12.q: To insure that a divisional vice president places appropriate focus on both the short-term and the long-term objectives of the division, the best approach would be to evaluate the vice president’s performance by using
o: return on investment (ROI) which permits easy and quick comparisons to other similar divisions
financial and nonfinancial measures, including the evaluation of quality, customer satisfaction, and market performance
residual income since it will eliminate the rejection of capital investments that have a return less than ROI but greater than the cost of capital
division segment margin or profit margin
3-12.e: Financial measures such as return on investment, residual income, and profit margins focus on the achievement of short-term objectives. Nonfinancial measures such as evaluation of quality, customer satisfaction and market performance focus on the achievement of long- term objectives. Evaluation of both financial and nonfinancial measures is called a balanced scorecard method of evaluation. The balanced scorecard includes both financial and nonfinancial measures in evaluating the overall contribution made by each unit to the achievement of company goals.
wrongWhy: Return on investment focuses on the achievement of short-term objectives.
Residual income focuses on the achievement of short-term objectives.
Segment margin and profit margin focus on the achievement of short-term objectives. 4.

3-12.q: Garret Corporation has decided to implement a balanced scorecard evaluation system and is considering several measurement factors. Customer returns Number of employees Manufacturing throughput time Number of manufacturing plants Return on investment Training hours Which of the following are most likely non-financial factors to be used in a balanced scorecard?
o: Customer returns, manufacturing throughput time, return on investment
Number of employees, number of manufacturing plants, training hours
Customer returns, manufacturing throughput time, training hours
Customer returns, number of employees, number of manufacturing plants
3-12.e: Customer returns, manufacturing throughput time, and training hours are all non-financial measurements that could be used in a balanced scorecard. Return on investment is a financial measurement, and number of manufacturing plants is not a meaningful metric.
wrongWhy: ROI is not a non-financial measure.
The number of manufacturing plants is not a meaningful metric, and is not likely to be used in a balanced scorecard.
The number of manufacturing plants is not a meaningful metric, and is not likely to be used in a balanced scorecard. 5.

3-12.q: A corporation has decided to perform a customer profitability analysis as a basis to prioritize next year’s sales activities. Select customers' information is shown below. Customer A Customer B Customer C Total corporate administrative expense is $500,000 and total corporate selling expense is $1,000,000. Based on the analysis, the company should
o: prioritize customer C because it results in the highest profit
focus on customer A because it has the highest profit margin of 50%
drop customer B because its gross profit is the lowest
calculate customer-specific selling expense to derive customer profitability
3-12.e: Customer profitability involves more than just the gross profit or gross profit margin for each customer. Other customer-level, or customer-specific, costs that would not exist if each customer's orders had not been received and fulfilled need to be considered, as well. Customer-specific expenses include the variable selling expense related to each individual customer's orders, and that amount should be deducted from each customer's gross profit margin to determine that customer's profitability. The customer-specific selling expense used should not include costs that would be incurred whether or not sales are made to that customer. It should include only costs incurred as a result of making sales to that specific customer, that is, costs that would not be incurred if that customer's orders were not received and fulfilled. Examples of customer- specific selling expenses are sales commissions paid and shipping-out expenses paid.
wrongWhy: Based on the information given, Customer C's gross profit is the highest of the three. However, customer profitability involves more than just the gross profit. Other customer-level, or customer-specific, costs that would not exist if that customer's orders were not received and fulfilled need to be considered, as well. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Based on the information given, Customer A's gross profit margin is the highest of the three. However, customer profitability involves more than just the gross profit or gross profit margin. Other customer-level, or customer-specific, costs that would not exist if that customer's orders were not received and fulfilled need to be considered, as well. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Based on the information given, Customer B's gross profit is the lowest of the three. However, customer profitability involves more than just the gross profit. Other customer-level, or customer-specific, costs that would not exist if that customer's orders were not received and fulfilled need to be considered, as well. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 6.

3-12.q: Paul Cooper, shipping manager for DFG Distributors, is responsible for managing the staff and all related transportation equipment to fill orders for bakery products from local retailers and deliver the products to those retailers. Which one of the following groups of three performance measures most likely would result in the highest level of goal congruence?
o: Customer satisfaction; elapsed time to complete an order; percentage of orders filled accurately
The percentage of orders filled on time; the percentage of orders filled accurately; average cost to fill and deliver an order
Labor cost per order; transportation cost per order; number of orders completed per day
Orders completed per employee per day; employee injuries per hour worked; number of vehicle accidents per year
3-12.e: The shipping manager is responsible for filling customer orders efficiently and accurately and delivering them quickly, at the lowest possible cost without negatively impacting customer service. Percentage of orders filled on time, percentage of orders filled accurately and average cost to fill and deliver an order are determined by comparing actual performance with established standards for those performance measures. These are appropriate measurements for aligning the shipping manager's goals with those of the company, because as the manager strives to attain those standards, he will be fulfilling his responsibilities.
wrongWhy: The shipping manager is responsible for filling customer orders efficiently and accurately and delivering them quickly, at the lowest possible cost without negatively impacting customer service. Customer satisfaction could mean customer satisfaction with the product as well as with the speed and accuracy of the delivery; and the shipping manager is not responsible for customer satisfaction with the product and so should not be evaluated on that. Elapsed time to complete an order does relate to the speed with which an order is filled. However, if there are no standards to compare the elapsed time against, the measurement is not meaningful.

The shipping manager is responsible for filling customer orders efficiently and accurately and delivering them quickly, at the lowest possible cost without negatively impacting customer service. Transportation cost per order is not something the shipping manager can control, as decisions about delivery vehicles are made at another level and fuel costs are not controllable. Number of orders completed per day is not meaningful because it does nothing to measure how quickly and accurately orders are filled after they are received.

The shipping manager is responsible for filling customer orders efficiently and accurately and delivering them quickly, at the lowest possible cost without negatively impacting customer service. Orders completed per employee per day is not meaningful because that can be affected by the number of orders received, and the number of orders received is not something the shipping manager can control. Though safety on the job is important, employee injuries per hour worked and number of vehicle accidents per year are not related to the shipping manager's primary responsibilities. 7.

3-12.q: The balanced scorecard provides an action plan for achieving competitive success by focusing management attention on a few performance indicators, called perspectives. Which one of the following is not one of the broad categories of performance indicators commonly focused on in the balanced scorecard?
3-12.e: The four broad categories of performance indicators focused on in the balanced scorecard are financial performance measures; customer measures; internal process measures; and learning and growth. Competitor business strategies is not a performance indicator that is focused on in the balanced scorecard.
wrongWhy: The four broad categories of performance indicators focused on in the balanced scorecard are financial performance measures; customer measures; internal process measures; and learning and growth.
The four broad categories of performance indicators focused on in the balanced scorecard are financial performance measures; customer measures; internal process measures; and learning and growth.
The four broad categories of performance indicators focused on in the balanced scorecard are financial performance measures; customer measures; internal process measures; and learning and growth. 8.

3-12.q: All of the following are considered appropriate goals for measuring a division manager's efficiency for a budgeting period except
o: a reduction in the organizational structure (fewer employees doing a given amount of work)
a targeted share of the market
3-12.e: Earnings per share projections and actual earnings per share are calculated for the company as a whole. There is no such thing as earnings per share for an individual division. Therefore, earnings per share projections are not considered an appropriate goal for measuring a division manager's efficiency for a budgeting period.
wrongWhy: A targeted reduction in the organizational structure for a division is considered an appropriate goal for measuring a division manager's efficiency for a budgeting period because the division's actual reduction accomplished can be compared with the targeted reduction to measure the manager's performance.

A targeted share of the market for a division is considered an appropriate goal for measuring a division manager's efficiency for a budgeting period because the division's actual share of the market can be compared with the targeted share of the market to measure the division manager's performance.

Budgeted operating income for a division is considered an appropriate goal for measuring a division manager's efficiency for a budgeting period because actual operating income can be compared with budgeted operating income to measure the division manager's performance. 9.

3-12.q: Pet Toys Inc. has four customers. Details on revenues and expenses are presented below. Customer A Customer B Customer C Customer D Which customer has the highest customer level operating profit per unit sold?
3-12.e: To calculate the customer-level operating profit per unit sold for each customer, consider only relevant revenues and costs. Relevant revenues and costs are those revenues and costs that would cease to exist if a customer were no longer making purchases. Once the customer-level operating profit is calculated, dividing each customer’s operating profit by the number of units sold to that customer results in the operating profit per unit sold for each customer. Relevant revenues and costs are: sales, cost of goods sold, delivery cost and order taking cost. Those revenues and costs would go away if the customer were no longer a customer. Administration, depreciation and utilities costs would continue. Thus, the customer-level operating profit per unit sold for each of the four customers is as follows: Customer A Customer B Customer C Customer D Sales $100,000 $150,000 $200,000 $250,000 Cost of goods sold 50,000 60,000 70,000 75,000 Delivery cost 10,000 25,000 30,000 50,000 Order taking 15,000 20,000 25,000 30,000 Customer-level operating profit $25,000 $45,000 $75,000 $95,000 Divided by number of units sold / 10,000 / 20,000 / 35,000 / 50,000 Equals: Customer-level operating $2.50 $2.25 $2.14 $1.90 profit per unit sold The customer with the highest customer-level operating profit per unit sold is Customer A.
wrongWhy: Customer D does not have the highest customer level operating profit per unit sold. See the correct answer for full calculations of all customers.
Customer C does not have the highest customer level operating profit per unit sold. See the correct answer for full calculations of all customers.
Customer B does not have the highest customer level operating profit per unit sold. See the correct answer for full calculations of all customers. 10.

3-12.q: Which one of the following best describes the performance elements contained in most balanced scorecards?
o: Neither financial performance measures nor non-financial performance measures
Only financial performance measures, and not non-financial performance measures
Only non financial performance measures, and non-financial performance measures
Both financial performance measures and non-financial performance measures
3-12.e: Most balanced scorecards include both financial performance measures and non-financial performance measures.
wrongWhy: Most balanced scorecards include both financial performance measures and non-financial performance measures.
Most balanced scorecards include both financial performance measures and non-financial performance measures.
Most balanced scorecards include both financial performance measures and non-financial performance measures. 11.

3-12.q: Management has been asked to evaluate the profitability of its customers and determine which one should be dropped. Each customer has a unique set of characteristics. While each purchases the same product from the manufacturer, sales representatives have developed specific contracted discounts with each customer. Additionally, each customer has factors such as specific handling and shipping needs that impact the variable costs incurred for each case sold. Data for each customer are presented below. Alpha Beta Theta Using customer profitability analysis, which customer should the manufacturer drop?
o: Drop Alpha because it has the lowest profitability
Drop Beta because it has the highest price discount
Drop Theta because it has the lowest revenues
Drop Beta because it has the highest variable cost per unit
3-12.e: Alpha's contribution margin is $149,850 ($386,100 − $236,250), and Alpha's contribution margin per unit is $5.55 ($149,850 ÷ 27,000 units sold). Beta's contribution margin is $151,200 ($511,200 − $360,000), and Beta's contribution margin per unit is $4.20 ($151,200 ÷ 36,000 units sold). Theta's contribution margin is $153,600 ($357,600 − $204,000), and Theta's contribution margin per unit is $6.40 ($153,600 ÷ 24,000 units sold). Alpha's contribution margin is the lowest of the three customers. Although the contribution margin per unit from sales to Alpha is higher than the contribution margin per unit from sales to Beta, the company's sales to Alpha are 25% lower than its sales to Beta. Therefore, if the company needs to drop one of the customers, it should drop Alpha.
wrongWhy: The size of the price discount is not the only consideration, and the contribution margin earned from sales to each customer is more important. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The revenues received from sales are not the only consideration, and the contribution margin earned from sales to each customer is more important. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The variable cost of sales is not the only consideration, and the contribution margin earned from sales to each customer is more important. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 12.

3-12.q: A sign of the successful implementation of a balanced scorecard is the presence of a cause- and-effect relationship. An example of this success for a hotel is meeting the target of
o: receiving more 5-star ratings from customers, which causes an increase in profit
increasing profit, which causes an increase in employee job satisfaction ratings
increasing employee training hours, which causes employee compensation to increase
decreasing a customer's check-in time, which causes an increase in the number of implemented employee suggestions
3-12.e: Receipt of more 5-star ratings from customers, for example on an Internet review site or in a tourist guide, will lead to a greater volume of reservations. A greater volume of reservations should lead to an increase in profit. Thus, there is a cause-and-effect relationship between receiving more 5-star ratings from customers and an increase in profit. The number of 5-star ratings received during a given period would be an effective Customer perspective key performance indicator for a balanced scorecard used by a hotel.
wrongWhy: Increasing profit has no cause-and-effect relationship to an increase in employee job satisfaction ratings. Increasing profit is not a key performance indicator at all but is rather the result of achieving Learning and Growth, Internal Process, Customer, and/or Financial perspective key performance indicators in a balanced scorecard system.

Increasing employee training hours has no cause-and-effect relationship to employee compensation increases. Increasing employee training hours may be an effective Learning and Growth key performance indicator in a balanced scorecard for a hotel because it could lead to greater efficiencies or better customer service and thus increased profitability. But an increase in employee compensation is not a result of increasing employee increasing employee training hours.

Decreasing a customer's check-in time has no cause-and-effect relationship to the number of implemented employee suggestions. Decreasing a customer's check-in time may be an effective Internal Process perspective key performance indicator in a balanced scorecard for a hotel because it could lead to decreased costs and thus increased profitability. But an increase in the number of implemented employee suggestions is not a result of decreasing customers' check-in times. 13.

3-12.q: An example of an item that would fall under the customer perspective on the balanced scorecard of an airline is
o: 90% of the flights will arrive on time
three new in-flight meals will replace existing offerings that are unpopular with customers
customers will have to wait no longer than 15 minutes to check their bags
3-12.e: Decreasing customer complaints by 10% is an example of a Customer perspective key performance indicator on a balanced scorecard for an airline.
wrongWhy: Improving the percentage of flights that arrive on time is an example of an Internal Process perspective key performance indicator on a balanced scorecard for an airline.

Three new in-flight meals replacing existing offerings that are unpopular with customers is an example of an Internal Process perspective key performance indicator on a balanced scorecard for an airline.
Improving customer wait times to check bags is an example of an Internal Process perspective key performance indicator on a balanced scorecard for an airline. 14.

3-12.q: Which one of the following statements about a balanced scorecard is incorrect?
o: It is directly derived from the scientific management theories
The notion of value chain analysis plays a major role in the drawing up of a balanced scorecard
It relies on the perception of the users with regard to service provided
It seeks to address the problems associated with traditional financial measures used to assess performance
3-12.e: The balanced scorecard is not derived from scientific management theories. Scientific management was a theory developed in the late 1800s. It focused primarily on the efficiency of individual workers and improving their productivity. Its principles were responsible for the development of "efficiency experts" and the use of time and motion analysis. The balanced scorecard is a strategic management tool which developed as a response to problems caused by evaluating managers only on the quarterly or annual financial performance of their business units. The balanced scorecard encourages managers to focus on elements of long-term success, which are non-financial and operational indicators, instead of only on short-term financial performance. It does this by rewarding managers for improvements in those elements of long- term success. Improvements in these non-financial measures provide the prospect of increased future economic value for shareholders. When managers are evaluated and rewarded based on non-financial indicators as well as financial indicators, it leads to long-term financial performance improvements. This again comes back to the idea of goal congruence and making sure that everyone is working toward the same goals.
wrongWhy: Value chain analysis is used to analyze the company's chain of activities for transforming inputs into the outputs that customers value. Value chain analysis can identify what activities increase value to the customers. The customer perspective is an important part of the non-financial indicators used in balanced scorecard evaluation. It is one of the four perspectives into which the balanced scorecard measurements used by companies typically fall. (The others are financial performance, innovation in internal business processes, and an organizational culture that supports learning and growth.) Developing the company’s customer perspective for a balanced scorecard involves measuring its success in the market segment(s) it wants to serve, such as share of the market, customer satisfaction, and quality goals. Value chain analysis provides important input to development of customer perspective metrics.

The balanced scorecard encourages managers to focus on elements of long-term success, which are non-financial and operational indicators, instead of only on short-term financial performance. It does this by rewarding managers for improvements in those elements of long- term success. One of the elements of long-term success is the customer's perspective with regard to service provided. Customer satisfaction is a vital part of the customer perspective, because if customers are not satisfied, they will take their business elsewhere. Customer satisfaction goals relate to how the company wants its customers to view it, i.e., the perceptions of its customers.

The balanced scorecard is a strategic management tool which developed as a response to problems caused by evaluating managers only on the quarterly or annual financial performance of their business units. 15.

3-12.q: David Burke is manager of claims processing for Continental Health Care System. His performance is evaluated using various measures agreed upon in advance with Diane Lewis, general manager. Lewis asked Burke to recommend several measures to evaluate the performance of his unit next year. Which one of the following performance measures would likely have the least positive effect on Burke’s motivation and performance?
o: Total monetary amount of claims processed per month
Percentage of claims processed accurately the first time
3-12.e: Total monetary amount of claims processed per month is not an accurate way of measuring the speed of processing because some claims will be for high amounts and others will be for lower amounts. That is not something the manager of claims processing can control, so the manager's performance should not be measured on it. Furthermore, evaluating the manager's performance on the monetary amount of claims processed could lead to the manager's giving priority to processing the larger claims. That would lead to larger claims being approved before smaller claims, and larger sums of cash would be paid out earlier to settle those larger claims. More cash paid out earlier would increase cash outflow, resulting in lower cash balances and higher cost of capital. Giving priority in processing would also result in poor customer service to the filers of the smaller claims.
wrongWhy: Average processing time per claim is something a manager can control through training. Measuring Burke's performance in this area would have the positive effect of encouraging him to keep processing time as low as possible.
Processing cost per claim is something a manager can control. Measuring Burke's performance in this area would have the positive effect of encouraging him to keep costs as low as possible.

Percentage of claims processed accurately the first time is something a manager can control through training. Measuring Burke's performance in this area would have the positive effect of encouraging him to improve the accuracy of his staff. 1.

3-12.q: The Balanced Scorecard was developed by:
3-12.e: BSC was developed by Robert Kaplan and David Norton (1992). It evolved from a measurement tool into a full strategic management framework. Their work addressed the limitations of purely financial performance measurement.

3-12.q: The Financial Perspective of the BSC is considered the:
o: Outcome/result of the other three perspectives
3-12.e: Financial perspective = Lag Indicator (outcome/result). If the Customer, Internal Process, and Learning perspectives are executed well, the financial results follow. The other three are Lead Indicators that predict future financial success.

3-12.q: The Learning & Growth perspective provides:
o: The Foundation — human, information, and organizational capabilities needed for all other perspectives
3-12.e: Learning & Growth is the Foundation of the BSC hierarchy. It builds employee skills, IT systems, and organizational culture that Enable better Internal Processes → better Customer outcomes → better Financial results.

3-12.q: A Critical Success Factor (Csf) is:
o: A key activity Essential to achieving strategic goals
3-12.e: Csf = key activities that are Essential to achieving strategic goals. They must be limited in number and directly tied to strategy. KPIs are the measurable indicators of how well CSFs are being achieved.

3-12.q: A Strategy Map in the BSC shows:
o: A visual cause-and-effect linkage between the four perspectives
3-12.e: Strategy Map = Visual representation showing Cause-And-Effect relationships from Learning & Growth → Internal Process → Customer → Financial. It demonstrates how improving foundational capabilities flows up to financial results.

3-13.q: Current Assets $320,000. Current Liabilities $160,000. Current Ratio =
3-13.e: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities = $320,000 / $160,000 = 2.0. The company has $2 of current assets for every $1 of current liabilities — a comfortable liquidity position.

3-13.q: The Quick Ratio differs from the Current Ratio because it:
o: Uses total assets instead of current assets
Excludes inventory and prepaid expenses — only uses most liquid assets
3-13.e: Quick Ratio (Acid-Test) = (Cash + Short-term investments + Accounts Receivable) / Current Liabilities. It Excludes Inventory and prepaid expenses because they may not be quickly convertible to cash. Stricter test of immediate liquidity.

3-13.q: Net Credit Sales $900,000. Average Accounts Receivable $75,000. Days Sales Outstanding =
3-13.e: Ar Turnover = $900,000 / $75,000 = 12 times. Dso = 365 / 12 = 30.4 days. On average, the company collects receivables in about 30 days.

3-13.q: ROE can be decomposed using DuPont analysis into:
o: Net profit margin × asset turnover × equity multiplier
Operating margin × debt ratio × interest coverage
3-13.e: DuPont: ROE = Net Profit Margin (NI/Sales) × Asset Turnover (Sales/Assets) × Equity Multiplier (Assets/Equity). This reveals whether ROE improvement comes from better margins, more efficient asset use, or higher financial leverage.

3-13.q: EBIT $500,000. Interest Expense $100,000. Times Interest Earned =
3-13.e: Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest Expense = $500,000 / $100,000 = 5.0x. The company earns its interest expense 5 times over — a healthy coverage ratio. Below 1.5x is typically concerning.

3-13.q: Inventory Turnover Decreases from 8x to 5x year-over-year. This most likely indicates:
o: Slow-moving, excess, or obsolete inventory building up
3-13.e: Falling inventory turnover = Slowing inventory movement. Inventory is sitting longer before being sold. Possible causes: declining demand, overbuying, obsolete products, or inefficient inventory management. Generally unfavorable.

3-13.q: A company's ROE increased from 15% to 20%. DuPont analysis shows net profit margin was unchanged and asset turnover declined. The increase was caused by:
3-13.e: If margin (profitability) and turnover (efficiency) are unchanged but ROE increased, the Equity Multiplier (Assets/Equity) must have increased — meaning More Debt was used to finance assets. This increases ROE through financial leverage but also increases financial risk.

3-13.q: Which ratio measures the percentage of sales remaining after deducting Only the cost of goods sold?
3-13.e: Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Net Sales = (Sales - COGS) / Sales. It shows the percentage remaining After COGS only — before operating expenses, interest, and taxes. Measures pricing power and production cost efficiency.

3-13.q: Debt-to-Equity Ratio = Total Debt / Total Equity. Total Debt = $400,000, Equity = $200,000. D/E =
3-13.e: D/E = $400,000 / $200,000 = 2.0. The company has $2 of debt for every $1 of equity. A higher D/E indicates more financial leverage — higher risk but potentially higher returns on equity.

3-13.q: Gross Margin % = Gross Profit / Net Sales. Sales = $500,000, COGS = $300,000. Gross Margin % =
3-13.e: Gross Profit = $500,000 − $300,000 = $200,000. Gross Margin % = $200,000 / $500,000 = 40%. This shows that 40 cents of every sales dollar remains after production costs — before S&A expenses.

3-13.q: Operating Profit Margin = Operating Income / Net Sales. If Operating Income = $80,000 and Sales = $500,000, Operating Margin =
3-13.e: Operating Margin = $80,000 / $500,000 = 0.16 = 16%. Shows what percentage of sales remains after All operating costs (COGS + S&A). More comprehensive than gross margin — includes selling and administrative expenses.

3-13.q: Return on Assets (ROA) = Net Income / Total Assets. NI = $60,000, Total Assets = $400,000. ROA =
3-13.e: ROA = $60,000 / $400,000 = 0.15 = 15%. Measures how efficiently the company uses All its assets (financed by both debt And equity) to generate profit. Different from ROE which measures return to equity holders only.

3-13.q: Asset Turnover = Net Sales / Average Total Assets. Sales = $900,000, Avg Assets = $600,000. Asset Turnover =
3-13.e: Asset Turnover = $900,000 / $600,000 = 1.5 times. Generates $1.50 in sales for every $1 of assets. Higher is better — indicates more efficient use of assets. Low asset turnover may indicate idle assets or poor capacity utilization.

3-13.q: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Ca = $280,000, Cl = $120,000. Working Capital =
3-13.e: Working Capital = $280,000 − $120,000 = $160,000. Positive working capital means current assets Exceed current liabilities — the company can cover short-term obligations with liquid assets. It measures operating liquidity.

3-13.q: Vertical (common-size) analysis expresses each income statement line as a percentage of:
o: Total Sales Revenue — shows the cost structure as % of revenue
3-13.e: Vertical analysis: express each income statement item as % of Net Sales. Example: COGS = 65% of sales, Gross Profit = 35%. This reveals the Cost Structure and enables comparison across companies of different sizes or across time periods regardless of absolute revenue.

3-13.q: Horizontal analysis compares:
o: Multiple companies at one point in time
The Same Company over time — showing percentage and dollar changes period to period
3-13.e: Horizontal analysis = Trend Analysis comparing the same company over multiple periods. Shows absolute changes ($) and percentage changes (%) in each line item from year to year. Identifies growth trends, improving or deteriorating margins, and unusual changes.

3-13.q: The Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio measures:
o: How many dollars investors Pay per dollar of earnings — market valuation multiple
3-13.e: P/E Ratio = Market Price per Share / EPS. Shows how much investors pay for each $1 of earnings. High P/E = investors expect High Future Growth. Low P/E = modest growth expectations or undervaluation. Useful for comparing companies in the same industry.

3-13.q: A High Debt-to-Equity ratio indicates:
o: The company has no financial risk
High Financial Leverage — more debt relative to equity, increasing return potential but also financial risk
3-13.e: High D/E = High Leverage. Advantages: interest is tax-deductible, amplifies returns on equity when profitable. Risks: higher interest expense, greater financial distress risk, more restrictive covenants. Lenders watch D/E to assess credit risk.

3-13.q: Free Cash Flow = Operating Cash Flow − Capital Expenditures. OCF = $200,000, CapEx = $80,000. FCF =
3-13.e: FCF = $200,000 − $80,000 = $120,000. Free Cash Flow is cash available after maintaining/expanding the asset base. It is available for dividends, debt repayment, acquisitions, or investment. Positive FCF indicates financial strength.

3-13.q: Net Profit Margin = Net Income / Net Sales. NI = $45,000, Sales = $300,000. Net Profit Margin =
3-13.e: Net Profit Margin = $45,000 / $300,000 = 0.15 = 15%. The Bottom Line profitability ratio — what percentage of each sales dollar is kept as net income after All costs (COGS, S&A, interest, and taxes). The most comprehensive profitability measure.
